title: Pain au lait
subtitle: Soft French milk bread rolls with a tender, buttery crumb
excerpt: Small, oval French bread rolls enriched with warm milk and butter to produce a soft, pillowy crumb and a thin, golden crust that tears rather than shatters. Slightly sweeter than a dinner roll, they are the standard bread roll in French bakeries for filling with ham or chocolate, or eating plain with good butter. The enrichment makes the dough take longer to rise than a lean dough, but the shaping is simple.
sourceNotes: Pain au lait is documented in French baking literature from at least the mid-nineteenth century as a standard enriched bread roll made with milk and butter. Le Cuisinier Europeen (1860, public domain), describes enriched milk breads as a common preparation in French bakeries. The small oval format and the combination of milk, eggs, and butter as enrichment are described in multiple French boulangerie manuals published before 1928.

content.text: The soft texture of pain au lait comes from the milk and butter enrichment, but that enrichment must be handled correctly. The butter goes in after the dough is kneaded, not before:
adding butter once the gluten is developed
gives a smooth, extensible dough rather than a greasy, weak one. The finished rolls should have a paper-thin crust that bends before it snaps.

items.name: Eggs
items.prepNote: plus 1 extra yolk for egg wash

content.text: Whisk the flour, yeast, salt, and sugar in a large bowl, keeping salt and yeast apart before mixing. Add the warm milk and the whole egg and mix to a rough dough. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic. Add the butter a few pieces at a time, kneading each addition in fully before adding the next. The dough will become very soft; continue kneading for 4 to 5 minutes until smooth and slightly tacky. Place in an oiled bowl, cover, and leave for 60 minutes until doubled.

Divide the dough into 10 equal pieces (about 90 g each). Roll each piece into a smooth oval, pressing out any large bubbles. Arrange on lined baking trays with space to expand. Cover and prove for 50 minutes until noticeably puffed.

Heat the oven to 185°C conventional (165°C fan). Beat the reserved egg yolk with a teaspoon of milk and brush gently over the proved rolls. Bake for 18 to 22 minutes until evenly golden. Cool on a rack for 10 minutes before eating.
